verses.commentary: The Gospel begins with a bold declaration. Not biography, not philosophy, but good news (euangelion, εὐαγγέλιον). Mark states his thesis: Jesus is both Messiah (Christ, the anointed king) and Son of God. The entire book will prove this claim.
Mark quotes Malachi 3:1, attributing it to Isaiah. The messenger prepares the way. John the Baptist fulfills ancient prophecy. God's plan unfolds across centuries.
Isaiah 40:3 quoted. A voice in the wilderness. The desert setting recalls Israel's formation under Moses. New exodus, new deliverance begins in the same unlikely place.
John appears in the wilderness. Baptism for repentance and forgiveness. A radical act: Israel, the covenant people, needs cleansing. John prepares hearts for the coming one.
Massive response. All Judea and Jerusalem come out. Confession precedes baptism. Public acknowledgment of sin, public commitment to change. The nation stirs with expectation.
John dresses like Elijah (2 Kings 1:8). Camel hair, leather belt, locusts, wild honey. The prophet Elijah must return before the great day of the Lord (Malachi 4:5). John is that forerunner.
John's message points beyond himself. One more powerful comes. Unworthy to untie his sandals (a slave's task). Radical humility. The greatest prophet deflects all attention to Jesus.
Water baptism versus Spirit baptism. John can only symbolize; Jesus will transform. The Holy Spirit brings the power of the age to come into the present.
Jesus comes from Nazareth, an obscure Galilean village. He submits to John's baptism. The sinless one identifies with sinners. Solidarity with humanity begins here.
Heaven torn open (schizo, σχίζω, violently ripped). The barrier between heaven and earth is breached. The Spirit descends like a dove. God's presence returns to his people.

The Father's voice. You are my Son (Psalm 2:7), whom I love (Genesis 22:2, the beloved son Isaac), with you I am well pleased (Isaiah 42:1, the Servant). Three Old Testament echoes: King, Sacrifice, Servant. Jesus' identity declared at the outset.
Immediately (euthys, εὐθύς, Mark's favorite word, used 42 times). The Spirit drives Jesus out. Not gently leads but forcefully expels. The same Spirit who descended now propels him to testing.

Forty days in the wilderness. Moses, Elijah, and Israel were tested for forty. Satan tempts Jesus. Wild animals present, yet angels minister. Second Adam in the wilderness, undoing the first Adam's failure.
After John's arrest, Jesus begins. One herald silenced, another begins. Galilee, not Jerusalem. The margins, not the center. God works from unexpected places.

The time is fulfilled. God's appointed moment arrives. The kingdom of God has come near. Repent and believe the good news. Four imperatives: time, kingdom, repentance, faith. This is the gospel in compressed form.
Jesus walks by the Sea of Galilee. Simon and Andrew cast nets. Ordinary fishermen, ordinary day. The call comes in the midst of regular work.
Follow me, and I will make you fishers of people. The call is to relationship (follow me) and mission (fishers of people). Jesus transforms identity and purpose.
Immediately they left their nets. Radical obedience. No negotiation, no delay. Nets represent livelihood, identity, family business. All abandoned for Jesus.
A little farther, James and John mending nets with their father Zebedee. Another ordinary scene about to be interrupted.
Immediately Jesus calls them. They leave their father in the boat with hired servants. Family ties severed. The call of Jesus supersedes even sacred obligations. Discipleship costs everything.
Capernaum becomes Jesus' base of operations. On the Sabbath he enters the synagogue. Jesus honors Jewish worship patterns while preparing to transform them.
People are astonished at his teaching. Authority, not like the scribes. Scribes quote authorities; Jesus speaks as authority. His word carries intrinsic power.
A man with an unclean spirit in the synagogue. Evil infiltrates religious spaces. The kingdom's arrival forces darkness into the open.
What do you want with us? The demon recognizes Jesus. I know who you are, the Holy One of God. Demons see what humans miss. Spiritual realities perceive spiritual authority.
Be quiet! Come out of him! Jesus rebukes and commands. He will not accept testimony from demons. His authority needs no validation from darkness.
The spirit shakes the man violently and comes out with a shriek. Deliverance is costly. Evil does not leave quietly. But Jesus' word prevails.
The people are amazed. A new teaching with authority! He commands even unclean spirits. Teaching and power are unified. Jesus doesn't just explain truth; he demonstrates it.
News spreads quickly throughout Galilee. The kingdom creates ripples. Word of mouth carries the gospel.
Jesus leaves the synagogue and goes to Simon and Andrew's house with James and John. Ministry moves from public to private, from synagogue to home.
Simon's mother-in-law is in bed with a fever. They immediately tell Jesus. Bringing needs to Jesus becomes the disciples' reflex.
Jesus takes her hand and helps her up. The fever leaves, and she begins to serve. Healing leads to service. Restoration has a purpose.
That evening after sunset (when Sabbath ends), the whole town brings the sick and demon-possessed. Word has spread. Need draws people to Jesus.
The whole city gathers at the door. Massive response. Jesus becomes the center of community hope.
Jesus heals many and drives out many demons. He will not let demons speak because they know who he is. Jesus controls the narrative of his identity. Revelation comes on his terms, his timeline.
Very early, while still dark, Jesus goes to a solitary place to pray. Intense ministry requires intense communion with the Father. Busy schedules demand, not replace, time with God.
Simon and companions go looking for Jesus. The crowds want more. Success creates pressure to repeat performance.
Everyone is looking for you! The unspoken request: stay here, capitalize on momentum, meet the demand.
Let us go somewhere else, to the nearby villages, so I can preach there also. That is why I have come. Jesus resists popular pressure. Mission, not success, determines movement. He knows his purpose.
Jesus travels throughout Galilee, preaching in synagogues and driving out demons. Itinerant ministry. The kingdom must spread beyond one location.
A man with leprosy comes, begs on his knees. If you are willing, you can make me clean. Faith and humility combined. He knows Jesus' power, questions only his willingness.
Jesus is indignant (some manuscripts say filled with compassion), reaches out and touches him. I am willing. Be clean! Touch the untouchable. Compassion crosses boundaries.
Immediately the leprosy leaves. Instant healing. Jesus' word accomplishes what it declares.
Jesus sends him away with a strong warning. See that you don't tell anyone. Jesus manages the spread of his fame. Messianic expectations could derail his mission.
Go, show yourself to the priest and offer the sacrifices Moses commanded. Jesus honors the law. The priests must witness and certify the healing. Legal process matters.

Instead he goes out and talks freely, spreading the news. Disobedience from gratitude. Jesus can no longer enter towns openly but stays outside in lonely places. Success becomes constraint. Yet people still come from everywhere. The kingdom cannot be contained.
